blocks.md: In 2012, LinkedIn had its password **hashes** stolen. The true scale only surfaced in 2016: 117 million accounts up for sale on the black market — followed by a cascade of break-ins on other sites, wherever victims had reused the same password.
Your password is the first lock on your digital life. But what actually makes a password strong? The answer surprises most people.

blocks.question: Which of these two passwords holds out longer against a brute-force attack?

choices.text: P@ss1 — short but packed with symbols
turtle beach windmill cactus — long but perfectly plain

blocks.reveal: Length wins: every added character multiplies the number of combinations to try. Four plain words take billions of times more guesses than a short word, symbols or not.

blocks.md: A good password really has nothing to do with fancy symbols: what matters is that it be **long, unpredictable and unique** to every account. An attacker churning through billions of combinations needs far longer to break a four-word phrase than `P@ss1`.
A **passphrase** made of several unrelated words (e.g. `giraffe-tunnel-storm-coffee`) is both stronger and easier to remember than a short word stuffed with symbols.

blocks.question: A forum you once had an account on gets breached. Your email and password leak. What do attackers do first?

choices.text: Sell the list and leave it at that
Try that email + password combo on banks, mailboxes and social networks
Nothing: a password alone is useless without the original site

blocks.reveal: It is fully automated: bots replay stolen credentials everywhere. One reused password, and your most sensitive account falls.

blocks.md: That mass replay has a name: **credential stuffing**. It is why uniqueness matters just as much as length. A serious site never stores your password in the clear anyway, only its **hash** (an irreversible fingerprint). Remembering dozens of unique passwords is impossible: that is exactly what a **password manager** is for, generating them, encrypting them and filling them in for you.

blocks.statement: If you forget your password, a serious site can simply email it back to you.
blocks.explanation: A serious site only stores the **hash**, an irreversible fingerprint: it does not know your password and can only let you create a new one. A site able to send your password back in the clear is storing it badly — be wary.

cards.back: App that generates, stores encrypted and auto-fills unique passwords, all protected by a single master password.
A password made of several words, so it is long and hard to crack yet easy to memorize.
Attack that replays credentials stolen elsewhere at scale, opening any account that reuses the same password.
An irreversible digital fingerprint of a piece of data; serious sites store the hash of your password, never the password itself.

blocks.md: Between 2013 and 2015, a Lithuanian scammer emailed fake invoices to Google and Facebook. Both tech giants paid — more than 100 million dollars in total. If their teams can be fooled by a simple email, nobody is too smart for phishing.

**Phishing** is an attempt to make you click, open an attachment or hand over a password by impersonating someone you trust: your bank, a delivery company, a colleague, a well-known service. It is now the most common way into an attack.

blocks.question: Which clue is the most reliable for unmasking a phishing message?

choices.text: Spelling mistakes
The real domain behind the link
A low-quality logo

blocks.reveal: Typos and logos are easy to fix — fraudulent messages are often flawless. The domain is the one thing that cannot be the real one: that is where the imposture shows.

blocks.md: The warning signs come up again and again: a **sense of urgency** (“your account is about to be suspended”), an odd sender address, a link whose real domain does not match the text shown, typos or a vague greeting (“Dear customer”), an unusual request for information or payment. Before clicking, hover over the link to reveal the real address, and check the **domain** (the name just before the first `/` that follows `https://`, for example `mybank.com`).

blocks.question: A pushy email seems to come from your bank and something feels off. The best reflex?

choices.text: Click the link to check, but type nothing
Reply to the email and ask for confirmation
Open the official site or app yourself

blocks.reveal: Never travel through a channel the attacker controls. By opening the official site under your own steam, you cut every string of the manipulation.

blocks.md: So when in doubt, never use the link or the phone number provided in the message: go to the official site under your own steam. Be extra careful when the message is personalized — your name, your job, your ongoing projects: that is **spear phishing**, targeted and far more believable.

Urgency is a **manipulation technique**, not a coincidence. The harder a message pushes you to act fast, the more you should slow down and verify through another channel (call the official number, open the app yourself).

blocks.explanation: The same trap arrives by SMS, phone call, social media or QR code. The channel changes, the mechanics stay the same: impersonate trust and push you to act fast.

cards.back: Fraudulent message imitating a trusted party to trap you: a click, an attachment or stolen credentials.
Targeted, personalized phishing aimed at one specific person, with realistic details that make it far more believable.
The name of a site (e.g. example.com); a slightly altered domain is a classic sign of a scam.

blocks.md: In 2019, Google published a study run with university researchers: a simple SMS code as a second factor blocked 100% of automated bot attacks on accounts. A second lock, even a basic one, changes the game completely.

**Two-factor authentication** adds a second proof of identity on top of the password. Even if your password leaks, the attacker is still stuck at the door: they are missing that second element. It is one of the most effective protections you can get for so little effort.

blocks.question: Which of these pairs makes real multi-factor authentication?

choices.text: Password + secret question
Password + code generated on your phone
Fingerprint + face recognition

blocks.reveal: Real MFA combines two different **families**. A password and a secret question are both “something you know”; a fingerprint and a face are both “something you are”.

blocks.md: These proofs are called **factors**, and they fall into three families: something you **know** (a password), something you **have** (a phone, a security key) and something you **are** (fingerprint, face). **MFA** combines at least two different families; **2FA** is simply the two-factor case.

blocks.question: Facing a fake login page (phishing), which second factor holds up best?

choices.text: The code received by SMS
A TOTP code from an app
A hardware security key (FIDO2)

blocks.reveal: SMS and TOTP codes can be retyped on a fake site by the victim themselves. A FIDO2 key checks the domain: it simply refuses to sign for an impostor.

blocks.md: Not all second factors are equal: an SMS code is better than nothing, but it remains vulnerable to SIM swapping. An authenticator app that generates a temporary code (**TOTP**) is safer, and a hardware security key (FIDO2) resists even phishing.
Whenever it is offered, choose an **authenticator app** or a **security key** over SMS codes. Keep your backup codes somewhere safe and offline so you never lock yourself out.

cards.back: Sign-in that requires at least two factors from different families (know, have, are).
The special case of MFA using exactly two factors.
One-time code valid for a few seconds, generated by a time-synchronized authenticator app.
Something that proves your identity: something you know, something you have or something you are.

blocks.md: May 2017: the **WannaCry** ransomware paralyzes more than 200,000 machines across 150 countries, British hospitals first among them. The patch blocking the exploited flaw had been available for two months. Machines that were up to date never felt a thing.

No software is ever perfect: **vulnerabilities** (flaws) are found in it all the time. Vendors then release a **patch**, a fix that plugs the hole. Installing updates quickly means closing the doors before anyone walks in.

blocks.question: A vendor releases a security patch. For the machines that do not install it, the immediate risk…

choices.text: Drops: the flaw is fixed
Rises: the flaw becomes public
Stays the same

blocks.reveal: Shipping a patch also announces the flaw to the whole world. Attackers dissect it and immediately go hunting for the stragglers.

blocks.md: Here is the key point: the moment a fix ships, the flaw becomes **public**. Attackers now know exactly what to aim at, and they scan the Internet looking for devices that have not been updated.

blocks.question: Your bet: the vast majority of intrusions exploit…

choices.text: Secret flaws nobody knows about (zero-days)
Already-fixed flaws, on systems that are not up to date

blocks.reveal: Why burn a rare, expensive weapon when millions of machines leave the door wide open? Zero-days are saved for high-value targets.

blocks.md: The vast majority of intrusions indeed do not exploit secret flaws but vulnerabilities that are **already fixed** and that the victims simply never installed. A flaw still unknown to the vendor, with no fix available, is called a **zero-day**: rarer, and far more expensive for the attacker. End-of-life software, which no longer receives patches, stays vulnerable forever.
Remember the logic: **known flaw + available patch + system not updated = the easiest target there is**. Turn on automatic updates everywhere you can.

cards.back: A software update that fixes a security flaw or a bug.
A weakness in software that can be exploited to compromise a system.
A vulnerability still unknown to the vendor, and therefore with no fix available at the time it is exploited.
Standardized public identifier assigned to a known vulnerability, so it can be referenced unambiguously.

blocks.md: Monday, 8:04 am. Your screen reads: “Your files have been encrypted. Pay to get them back.” Photos, documents, accounting — everything is locked. Only one question still matters: is there a copy the attacker could not reach?

A **backup** is a copy of your data that you can restore if the original is lost. Drive failure, theft, accidental deletion, or **ransomware** (malware that encrypts your files to demand a ransom): with no copy, the data is gone for good.

blocks.question: You keep three copies of your data… all of them in the same house. Which scenario still loses everything?

choices.text: None: three copies are enough
A fire, a burglary or a flood
A single drive failing

blocks.reveal: A local disaster takes out everything stored in the same place, no matter how many copies. One of them has to live somewhere else.

blocks.md: The **3-2-1 rule** sums up a sound strategy: **3** copies of your data in total, on **2** different types of media (e.g. internal drive + external drive), with **1** of them kept **off-site** (in another location or in the cloud). Different media keep a single failure from destroying everything; the off-site copy protects against fire, theft or flooding.

blocks.question: Your backup lives on an external drive permanently plugged into the PC. Ransomware strikes. Your copy…

choices.text: Is safe: it is a separate drive
Gets encrypted too

blocks.reveal: Ransomware encrypts everything the machine can reach — external drives and network shares included. A copy that is always plugged in is not isolated.

blocks.md: That is why, ideally, at least one copy is **offline** or write-once: ransomware can only encrypt what it can reach.
A backup you have never tested is not a backup. **Check regularly that you know how to restore** a file: plenty of people discover a corrupted or empty copy on the very day they need it.

blocks.statement: My files are synced to the cloud, so I have a backup.
blocks.explanation: Sync replicates your mistakes too: a file deleted or encrypted by ransomware is instantly overwritten everywhere. A real backup is a separate copy, ideally with version history.

cards.back: Copy of data kept separately, meant to restore the originals if they are lost.
Three copies, on two types of media, one of them kept off-site.
Malicious software that encrypts your files and demands a ransom to make them accessible again.
Copy stored in a different location from the original, out of reach of the same local disaster.

blocks.md: An airport, a one-hour wait. Your phone picks up “Airport_Free_WiFi” and “Free_Airport_WiFi”. One is official; the other may be broadcasting from a backpack three seats away. Nothing on your screen can tell them apart.

The free Wi-Fi in a café or an airport is convenient, but you control neither the network nor who else is connected to it. The good news: thanks to **HTTPS** encryption, the content of your exchanges with websites can no longer be read so easily. The real danger has moved elsewhere.

blocks.question: So, on public Wi-Fi today, what is danger number one?

choices.text: Your passwords travel in the clear and are easy to read
A fake network set up by an attacker
Someone stealing your bandwidth

blocks.reveal: HTTPS closed the door on passively reading your content. The modern attack is to become the network itself: you connect straight to the attacker.

blocks.md: That **rogue access point**, or **evil twin**, is a fake network with a believable name (“Free_Cafe_WiFi”) set up so that you connect to it. The attacker can then redirect you to fake login pages, rig a **captive portal** (the landing page that asks you to accept the terms) or attack a poorly protected device.

blocks.question: You only browse HTTPS sites on that Wi-Fi. What does the network still see?

choices.text: Nothing at all
The domain names you visit
The full content of your pages

blocks.reveal: The content is encrypted, but some metadata stays visible — including the domains you contact. The network knows where you go, not what you do there.

blocks.md: Indeed, even over HTTPS some metadata stays visible, such as the **domain names** you visit. A **VPN** (virtual private network) creates an encrypted tunnel between your device and a trusted server, hiding those traces from the local network.

Check the **exact network name (SSID)** with the staff, turn off auto-connect to open Wi-Fi networks, and avoid sensitive operations if you have no VPN. An HTTPS padlock is no proof that the network is honest.

cards.back: Fake Wi-Fi network imitating a legitimate one to intercept or trap the devices that connect to it.
Encrypted tunnel between your device and a trusted server, which shields your traffic from the local network.
Landing page a public Wi-Fi forces you through before granting Internet access, which can be imitated to set a trap.
Public name of a Wi-Fi network, easy for an attacker to copy in order to create a believable fake.

title: HTTPS and the padlock: what it really guarantees

blocks.md: In 2019, the FBI issued an unusual alert: stop trusting the padlock. Phishing sites now sport perfectly valid HTTPS certificates — precisely because the public believes padlock = trustworthy site.

The little **padlock** in the browser tells you the page is using **HTTPS**, that is, HTTP protected by **TLS**, the layer that encrypts the traffic. That matters, but many people read far more into it than it actually says.

blocks.question: What does the padlock actually guarantee?

choices.text: The site is honest and vetted
The connection is encrypted, to the domain shown
The site has been audited by the browser

blocks.reveal: The padlock speaks about the **connection**, not the site. Encrypted transport and domain identity: nothing more — and that is already a lot.

blocks.md: The padlock guarantees two things. First, the **confidentiality and integrity of the transport**: what travels between you and the site is encrypted and cannot be read or altered along the way. Second, the **identity of the domain**: thanks to a **certificate** validated by a **certificate authority**, you really are talking to the domain shown in the address bar, not to an impostor.

blocks.statement: Getting an HTTPS certificate is expensive and requires deep vetting — out of a scammer's reach.
blocks.explanation: Some authorities issue free certificates within minutes, on simple proof that you control the domain. That is excellent for the web's security — and says nothing about a site's honesty.

blocks.md: So the padlock says **nothing about the honesty of the site's owner**. A phishing site can perfectly well obtain a free certificate and display a flawlessly valid padlock: the padlock proves “this connection is private”, never “this site is trustworthy”.
The padlock is **not** a trust badge. Always check **the domain itself**: `paypa1.com` can have a spotless padlock and still be a scam.

cards.back: Encrypted version of the HTTP web protocol, protected by TLS.
Protocol that encrypts the connection and authenticates the server's domain.
Electronic proof binding a domain to an encryption key, presented by the site to the browser.
Trusted organization that issues and signs the certificates vouching for a site's domain.

title: Screen lock, permissions and mobile privacy

blocks.md: Your phone has just slipped out of your pocket on the metro. In someone else's hand, that slab holds your messages, your photos, your email, your banking — and everything needed to reset almost all your passwords. What happens next comes down to two settings.

Those two settings are a solid **screen lock** and serious management of the **permissions** you grant to apps. The first protects the device itself — the key to your digital life; the second limits what each app gets to see of it.

blocks.question: A thief grabs your locked phone, takes it apart and wires the memory chip to a computer. He reads…

choices.text: Everything: the memory is right there in front of him
Nothing usable: encrypted data
Only the photos

blocks.reveal: While locked, a modern smartphone keeps its data encrypted. Without the passcode, the memory yields nothing but unreadable gibberish — even pulled out of the device.

blocks.md: The lock screen really is more than a visual curtain. On a modern smartphone, while it is locked the data is protected by **encryption at rest**: without the passcode, a thief cannot read it, even by taking the device apart. Biometrics (fingerprint, face) conveniently unlock the key, but your **passcode** remains the foundation of that security — make it long and set the screen to lock quickly on its own.

blocks.question: A flashlight app asks for access to your contacts and your location. That is…

choices.text: Normal: most apps ask for those
A red flag: it has no need for them

blocks.reveal: A request unrelated to the app's purpose is the mark of data harvesting. The right rule has a name: least privilege.

blocks.md: On the app side, that is exactly the **principle of least privilege**: a flashlight has no need whatsoever for your contacts or your location. Grant permissions sparingly, prefer “allow only while using the app”, and review regularly what has access to the camera, the microphone or your location, including in the background.
Do some housekeeping: revoke the permissions of apps you no longer use, and turn on **remote locate / remote wipe** in case the phone is lost or stolen.

cards.back: Passcode, pattern or biometric protection that blocks access to the device and brings encryption at rest into play.
Encryption of the data stored on the device, unreadable without the unlock code.
Access an app asks for in order to reach a resource: contacts, camera, microphone, location…
Granting only the access strictly required, so as to limit the damage in case of abuse or a flaw.
